data.title: AXT: A $3 Billion Valuation For A $88 Million Revenue Reality Check
data.description:
This is my first coverage of AXT Inc. (AXTI). In this article, I want to analyze how AXT is benefiting from the growth of AI datacenters and also the hype surrounding it. I will talk about rising demand for its indium phosphide substrates, the latest earnings report, and lastly the valuation. While bulls argue that indium phosphide is a critical material, I tend to agree, but I strongly disagree with the current valuation, as the stock rose over 3,000% from its 52-week lows over the last year, according to my calculations. I believe this to be excessive and the result of hype, and therefore I am rating it as a ''Sell''.

The Demand for High-Speed Data Transfer

To understand the hype surrounding AXT, we first need to analyze their business to figure out what they do and how defensible is their moat. So, data is transmitted as light (photons) through fiber optic cables. This light is created by the indium phosphide diodes, using electrical current from silicon chips, to create photons that transmit data through the fiber optic cables. Indium phosphide is much more efficient compared to older materials like gallium arsenide. For further detail, I recommend reading this article. In a high-tech AI chip, requiring fast data transmission speeds, this is essential, creating high demand for AXT's product – indium phosphide.

As hyperscalers build new datacenters, the demand for indium phosphide should also rise. As I discussed in previous articles about the crypto miners pivoting to HPC, this trend is really strong, and data center vacancy rates are at record lows. Hyperscalers also plan to invest more than half a trillion dollars into AI development in 2026. These macro trends indeed suggest the demand for indium phosphide will rise. But the real question is if AXT can grow into its current valuation, before supply eventually catches up with demand, and indium phosphide slowly gets commoditized? I will try to provide my estimates in the valuation section of the article.

Q4 2025 Earnings and Backlog

To understand the recent performance of the stock, we need to look at the Q4 earnings report. AXT reported revenue of $23 million, which seems minuscule since we are amidst the AI boom, and there is an insatiable demand for new datacenters and Nvidia’s hardware. What’s more, the full-year 2025 revenue contracted to $88.3 million compared to $99.4 million in 2024. This certainly cannot explain the bullish price movement the stock experienced.

One has to look at the company's forward-looking statements regarding Indium Phosphide. Management noted that ''our backlog for indium phosphide wafers have reached a new high of over $60 million. As we mentioned last quarter, customers are planning for longer lead time by placing longer-term orders and giving us more visibility into their expected demand.''

In the earnings call they also stated that ''revenue related to the data center market in China is expected to grow by more than 60% in Q1 over Q4.'' As a result, AXT plans to double its indium phosphide manufacturing capacity by the end of 2026.

These are certainly good news, but can it really justify a market cap of more than $3 billion? Let’s not forget that last quarter Nvidia (NVDA) brought in approx. $68 billion in revenues while AXT made $23 million in revenues. How come? Well, the answer is that only a minuscule amount of indium phosphide is actually needed even in high-end diodes.

If we assume that demand for indium phosphide will linearly scale with the datacenter buildup, Nvidia would need to scale their revenues 10x, for AXT to reach $230 million in quarterly revenues, based on the Q4 earnings report. Is this reasonable to expect? No, not in my opinion.

Valuation

To truly understand how excessive the current valuation is, one needs to look at the future indium phosphide market size estimates. One of the more bullish reports I found estimates that the total market size will reach $586 million by 2033. If AXT manages to take 40% market share, which is in my opinion an overly bullish estimate, they would make $234 million in annual revenues. For a material manufacturer, where the main moat is the manufacturing difficulty of the indium phosphide, I can’t pen out more than a 20% net margin. At such a margin, they would make approx. $45 million in annual net income. At the current market cap, AXT would have a 74x PE ratio.

If we are even more bullish and the market size reaches 2x, what the aforementioned analysts are estimating, to a $1.17 billion, while keeping the other variables the same, we arrive at the 37x PE multiple in 2033.

To conclude the valuation chapter, even at the most extremely bullish projections, AXT would only grow into the current valuation. As such, I cannot recommend buying the stock.

Risks

I believe that there are two main risks to my thesis. The first one is that if one wants to short the stock, one needs to remember a saying ''Markets can stay irrational longer than an investor can stay liquid.'' The valuation of more than $3 billion is excessive for this stock, but compared to the market cap of giant hyperscalers, this market cap is negligible. As long as sector hype remains, investors will look for new ''cheap'' opportunities, and as long as this stock keeps rising, the hype cycle could fuel itself.

The second reason is more technology based. Indium phosphide is currently more expensive than gallium arsenide, and as such is used only in high-performance niches. On the other hand, gallium arsenide is used in lower-end electronics, where high speeds are not mandatory. If indium phosphide manufacturing gets cheaper, due to cost-scaling laws, we could see that it starts to take some market share away from gallium arsenide, increasing the total market size. Still, I accounted for the greater market size in the valuation section, and the stock would still barely grow into the current valuation.

Conclusion and Plan

Based on the valuation metrics, this stock would be a perfect short. But we cannot forget that this company is not a steel manufacturer, but is in the most hyped-up sector of the market – AI. As such, opening a short position can be dangerous. Although the stock is perhaps overextended, the momentum is still strong, trading above both short and long simple moving averages. I would recommend waiting for some sideways price action, when buyers get exhausted before opening a short position. Still, even once that happens, I would only initialize a small to mid-sized position.

data.title: Vertiv: The Hyperscaler Spending Trade
data.description:
With the market back in risk-on mode, I am initiating coverage on Vertiv Holdings Co (VRT), a prime picks and shovels play on the AI data center buildout.

Despite the 25% jump in one day after reporting a spectacular backlog increase in Q4 2025, the stock didn't bother to backfill that gap. In fact, despite the broader selloff driven by the Hormuz blockade, this stock is trading at all-time highs as I'm typing.

Part of this outperformance is the inclusion in the S&P 500 in March, and a recent wave of new analyst coverage and upgrades.

Even though the stock is not cheap, trading at 47x forward earnings, I see further room for multiple expansion given that the inflection point occurred only a few months ago. I discuss more about this inflection point below, but first, I will provide a quick introduction to this company.

Company Overview

Here is the business overview section from the latest 10-K:

We design, manufacture, sell, install, maintain, and service critical digital infrastructure technologies and rapidly deployable customized solutions to meet the specific business requirements and needs of a diverse group of customers.

Now, in plain English, the company sells the physical infrastructure that lets data centers run. That mainly includes power equipment, cooling equipment, switchgear, busbar, racks, modular systems, software, and they also provide services like installation and maintenance.

The company mainly works with hyperscalers and neocloud companies. I say mainly because the March 2026 fact sheet says about 85% of fiscal 2025 revenue came from data centers.

Furthermore, as seen above, most of the revenue last year came from selling products (80%), with services and spares representing only 20% of total revenue.

The general idea of the bull case can be summarized in a few words: AI racks are getting (and will keep getting) more power-hungry.

The chart below shows the trend in kW/rack for the past few years:

People significantly smarter than me expect this trend to continue in the future:

Therefore, more power per rack (and more heat per rack) pulls more of Vertiv’s portfolio into each deployment.

In other words, Vertiv is a prime picks and shovels play on the AI data center buildout.

The State Of The AI Data Center Buildout

The main pillar of the bull case on Vertiv is the AI CapEx supercycle. As long as hyperscalers are willing to throw cash into the AI data center build furnace, Vertiv is well-positioned to benefit.

The chart below shows the CapEx spending of the four hyperscalers in 2025 vs the 2026 forecast.

In this year alone, Microsoft, Amazon, Alphabet, and Meta plan to spend about $635 billion on data centers, chips, and other AI infrastructure.

That's a 66% yoy increase.

Based on what I'm reading, I think we are not even close to peak spending. The key catalyst seems to be the growth in AI inference workloads, which is projected to increase at a CAGR of 35% until 2030.

Bottom line: the AI bears / AI doomers are not looking at the data, in my view. To be crystal clear, I agree with them on the AI bubble risk. Where I disagree is on the timing of when the AI bubble will pop. I think we are a few years away, not a few months.

Like Soros once said:

When I see a bubble forming, I rush in to buy, adding fuel to the fire...

That is not irrational.

Strong Backlog Growth

The stock jumped 25% on the day the company reported Q4 2025 earnings.

Prior to the Q4 2025 earnings report, bears argued that Vertiv was benefiting from AI hype but still had the normal risks of an industrial supplier, namely the overreliance on same-quarter orders.

That view changed pretty sharply.

The Q4 report showed organic orders up about 252% yoy. The overall backlog at the end of Q4 2025 was $15B, up 109% yoy. Most of this backlog is expected to ship within the next 12 to 18 months, which prompted the Street to (drastically) move its revenue models after the print.

During a recent conference, the CFO revealed a key piece of information about the new orders. I include the full excerpt below:

What we're starting to see is the back half of that filling up more. So the back half of the 12 to 18 months filling up more in terms of orders that we're getting to elongate that what we call fill up of the backlog. So when we say the orders that we saw in the fourth quarter, especially in December, are filling in that back half of the backlog.

His remarks suggest that visibility is extending deeper into late 2026 and into 2027 rather than merely filling the next few quarters.

On top of that, the company reported a book to bill ratio of roughly 2.9x. For readers new to this metric, anything above 1 means demand is coming in faster than the company is delivering it.

As for the FY 2026 guidance, I considered including below an excerpt from the latest investor presentation:

Everything up vs 2025 is music to my ears. Let's now focus on the noise.

Risks

Europe is anemic with AI.

Don't take it from me, take it from no other than Alex Karp during the Q4 2024 earnings call:

We see — as much as I personally care about the broader West, including continental Europe, despite our best efforts and working out every day, it’s anemic

What does this mean for Vertiv?

Well, most of the growth is driven by the Americas segment. In Q4, the Americas segment revenue was up 46%, while APAC fell about 9% and EMEA fell about 14%.

This creates a concentration risk. In fact, the guidance for 2026 assumes Americas organic growth in the high-30% range, APAC in the mid-20% range, and EMEA flat to down mid-single digits.

Notably, the guidance for Q1 2026 implies a mid-20% decline in sales in the EMEA region.

One year later, it appears that the AI data center buildout on the other side of the Atlantic still remains anemic.

Another risk that is worth noting is the fact that Nvidia’s (NVDA) upcoming Vera Rubin platform would not require water chillers for data centers. In fact, on the day Jensen Huang announced this, Vertiv was down about 2%.

This was a hot topic in the last Q&A. Basically, management said that higher-temperature GPU operation may indeed reduce some chiller needs, but it does not eliminate the need to remove heat from the system. In fact, they argued that future data centers will use a complex mixed cooling architecture, which appears to be a tailwind for the company:

So all in all, we see that design continues to be mixed. If anything, this complicates the thermal chain and this complexity is something that we like as someone who has got the entire portfolio, we certainly are perfectly positioned to support our customers.

Until I see a significant drop in backlog growth, I am not overly concerned about this.

An even bigger risk that makes me quite worried is a new wave of analysts who are basically saying that the hyperscalers will struggle to burn through their CapEx plans in 2026 due to the bottleneck in power.

Counterintuitively, the main pillar of the bull case (i.e., AI racks are getting more power-hungry) is also the main pillar of the bear case, as data centers may struggle to find available power for their racks.

If this risk materializes this year, that may push Vertiv's revenue to the right, which is likely going to lead to a selloff of the same magnitude as the pop post Q4 2025 earnings.

Looking Ahead

I admit that the risk above is enough to keep me up at night (sort of). However, last year, I still remember some articles arguing the exact same risk. In fact, here is a chart (particularly the subtitle) that made me sweat more than I want to recognize last year:

Despite my strong buy rating on Vertiv, I would keep a very close eye on any rumors, analyst commentary/notes, leaks, earnings calls Q&A sessions, etc., regarding any signs of delays in the buildout plans of the hyperscalers and the neoclouds.

So far, I don't see anything concerning. However, absence of evidence is not evidence of absence.

Keeping that risk in mind, I’m bullish on Vertiv, a prime picks and shovels play on the AI data center buildout, and I own the stock in my portfolio to show some skin in the game.

data.title: Applied Digital: A Winner In The AI CapEx Boom
data.description:
Applied Digital Corporation (APLD) beat estimates for its third fiscal quarter on April 8, 2026, on both the top and the bottom line as the company published strong traction regarding its Data Center ramp. The AI infrastructure enterprise, which is tapping into the rapidly expanding market for GPUs, has seen explosive growth in its top line in the current fiscal year, including in its last quarter: in Q3'26, Applied Digital reported more than 139% year-over-year revenue growth.

However, Applied Digital also published a massive increase in its operating and net losses, which are related to the buildout of its Data Center footprint. Given the secular nature of Data Center CapEx spending in the market, I believe Applied Digital is an attractive AI infrastructure investment for long-term investors, even at a higher-than-average valuation. Shares surged 10% after the earnings report, but I believe this is still a good opportunity for investors to buy into this AI growth play.

Previous rating

I rated shares of Applied Digital a strong buy in my last coverage in October of last year—A Strong Play On The AI Boom—due to a robust demand outlook for the GPU market, which was set to benefit AI infrastructure providers in general. The company also signed a massive $5.0B investment deal with an Australian asset management company at the time, which gave Applied Digital additional resources to expand its Data Center footprint. While shares of APLD are expensive relative to other neoclouds, I like the secular nature of CapEx spending and the ongoing boom in the AI market…all of which is going to support, in my opinion, Applied Digital's expansion in the server market.

Applied Digital beats estimates for Q4’25

The AI infrastructure enterprise beat bottom- and top-line estimates on April 8, 2026, by decent margins: it reported $0.09 per share in normalized earnings for the third fiscal quarter, which beat the consensus figure by a massive $0.30 per share. The top line, which was boosted by higher Data Center utilization, came in at $126.6M and exceeded the average prediction by $48.2M. Despite the much better-than-expected earnings report card for Q3'26, shares of Applied Digital soared immediately after earnings by 10%, which was followed by a day of profit-taking on Thursday.

Data Center buildout will benefit Applied Digital’s core business

Applied Digital, which pivoted from cryptocurrency mining activities to high-performance computing in 2022, has seen a major uplift in its financial performance in the current fiscal year. The company builds massive AI factories to offer hyperscalers power-dense AI infrastructure that they need to run compute-intensive AI applications.

For example, the firm's AI factory campuses in North Dakota handle hundreds of megawatts of power and use proprietary liquid cooling systems to support hyperscalers' insatiable need for GPU compute. Applied Digital, earlier this year, in January, broke ground on the new Delta Forge 1-branded AI factory campus in Texas, which is designed for a 430 MW total utility footprint, with the initial buildout targeting 300 MW of critical IT load.

Applied Digital generated $126.6M of revenues from its Data Center operations in the third fiscal quarter, highlighting 139% year-over-year growth. Since the company is focused on ramping up its Data Center footprint in North Dakota, Applied Digital increased its operating losses by a factor of 4.5X, while its net losses (before consideration of non-controlling interest and preferred stock payments) doubled to $70.6M.

The AI infrastructure company currently has 700 MW of critical IT load under construction in North Dakota and Texas, which is expected to gradually come online in the next two years, supporting additional revenue gains.

According to Mitsubishi UFG, a Japanese investment bank, the five largest hyperscalers in the U.S.—Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL), Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), and Oracle Corporation (ORCL)—are set to drastically ramp up their CapEx programs this year to tap into the rapidly expanding market for generative AI models. How rapidly this market is evolving was also recently displayed by Broadcom Inc. (AVGO), announcing a massive deal with Google and Anthropic to help the company ramp up its upcoming gen-AI models, Claude 5 and 6: Triad AI Deal Is A Game Changer.

The investment powerhouse expects just these 5 companies to spend more than $600B on AI-related investments this year—showing 36% year-over-year growth, with Amazon alone expected to account for about $200B of this investment volume, according to Amazon’s own guidance: CapEx Shock.

Rising debt to finance buildouts may be a risk

Applied Digital is growing aggressively, in part through the use of other people's money. As of the end of the third quarter, Applied Digital had $2.6B in financial (long-term) debt on its balance sheet, which marked a massive 3.8X factor increase compared to the end of FY 2025 (May 2025). Other companies that are also growing rapidly—and that switched their business models over to high-performance computing and GPU leases—are Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS) and CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV). CoreWeave currently has the most aggressive CapEx plans, as the company expects to spend between $30B and $35B on its Data Center expansion this year.

Applied Digital’s valuation premium

As a key AI infrastructure player with a growing physical footprint, Applied Digital can be compared to other AI infrastructure companies such as Nebius or CoreWeave, despite them being chiefly GPU-as-a-service platforms. Shares of Applied Digital have a forward price-to-revenue ratio of 12.1X, which compares against P/S ratios of 3.9X for Nebius and 2.2X for CoreWeave.

NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), which is in a valuation league of its own as the largest GPU designer and chipmaker in the industry, is priced at a forward revenue multiplier of 9.4X. Applied Digital so far has the lowest revenue volume of all AI companies in the industry group—just about $550M on a forward basis—but the highest price-to-revenue ratio.

Applied Digital only has a market cap of $7.2B, making it by far the smallest company discussed here, but it may be this small size that gives APLD all the upside: smaller companies have a lot more potential to expand their market caps than large companies, and with Applied Digital expanding its Data Center footprint, I would not be surprised to see a serious ramp-up of the firm's revenues in the next several years (+100% annually).

Risks with Applied Digital

Applied Digital is expanding rapidly via the previously discussed Data Center footprint expansion in North Dakota (the Polaris Forge 1 and 2 campuses) and in Texas (the Delta Forge campus). The company recently completed a $2.15B Senior Secured Notes offering to expand Polaris Forge 2, which has significantly increased the firm’s debt load. Secondly, Applied Digital will remain dependent on the pace of CapEx spending in the Data Center industry, which is outside the company's control. What would get me to change my opinion on Applied Digital is if the AI infrastructure enterprise were to fail to rein in its operating and net losses following the completion of its current Data Center projects (post-2027).

Final thoughts

Applied Digital crushed estimates for earnings and revenues on April 8, 2026, amid accelerating momentum in its core Data Center business. Most importantly, the strong outlook for CapEx spending in the server industry in general is a major growth driver for Applied Digital, especially with the Polaris Forge 1 and 2 campuses ramping up in the next two years in Dakota, as well as the new AI factory campus that is being built in Texas. While Applied Digital is expensive compared to other AI infrastructure and GPU-as-a-Service plays such as Nebius and CoreWeave, the firm is much smaller than its rivals and therefore has much more potential to grow into a higher market valuation long term. I see CapEx trends as fully intact and would venture to say that Applied Digital will be a major winner of the CapEx cycle in the server market in 2026 and beyond.

data.title: Canadian Pacific: More Tricky Than Ratings Suggest
data.description:
Notice: Except where noted, all amounts are in millions of Canadian dollars.

Infrastructure and railroad companies are part of what I like to cover in my articles. That makes Canadian Pacific (CP:CA) (CP) interesting for me to cover. However, while many ratings at the time of writing are positive—both Wall Street and SA analysts are positive—historical trends suggest that this company may be trickier than expected or forecasted by analysts.

In this article, I'll establish basic coverage for Canadian Pacific. This involves looking at company operations, fundamentals, upsides, and risks. I invest in and cover a lot of infrastructure and railway companies—everything from the manufacturing of rolling stock and locomotives to signalling, to the rails themselves (the materials) and also the companies that use them, where they are publicly traded. While I can easily find the companies that manufacture the materials and products in Europe, investing in the actual infrastructure companies that run traffic on the rails is much harder here because most of these are not publicly traded.

So it's nice to see companies like CP that are even technically investable.

CP has a lot going for it. It's BBB+ rated, it has a well-covered dividend, and it has one of the most extensive and important rail networks in NA. The fundamental case for railway transport, where it is possible, is a very easy one to make. It is significantly cheaper and safer than trucking or flight transport, it's obviously environmentally superior in every way, and it has the advantage of mass capacity and significant reliability. A single railway can move as many goods and people as a 10-lane highway, and despite some accidents on the rails, railway travel and transport remain one of the safest modes of transportation around.

In nations where railways are built out, meaning where high-speed rail is a thing, short-haul flights are, in turn, not a thing or are sometimes being competed out of existence. A good example is here in Sweden. If I wanted to fly from Gothenburg to Stockholm, I don't just have to arrive at the airport early, check in, check out, and handle the airport in Stockholm (with all the fees associated and all of the transport), but I also usually pay twice what a railway ticket costs. With rail, I literally step on board in Gothenburg Central and step off in Stockholm about 3 hours later—no check-in fuss, and I'm already in the middle of town.

Railway travel and transport, where it works, is superior to most other forms of transport. It's why I use it whenever I can.

I therefore inherently like railway companies.

Let's see about this one.

Canadian Pacific Kansas City Limited - Upsides and operations

CP, or as they are usually called, CPKC, is a unique player. It's unique because it operates the only and first transnational railway line connecting Mexico to Canada through the US. This company is a merger, as the name suggests, of Canadian Pacific and Kansas City Southern, and a merger that the mergees hoped would make it a globally-dominating force in trade (for its area).

The advantages of this corporate structure that CPKC has adopted are several, but the biggest by far is the entire elimination of interchanges, which were required previously. No longer does the cargo need to be moved from one to another carrier/railway. Instead, the entire line is running on a single network. This has already proven to eliminate transit times by upward of two entire days between the Midwest, the UK, and Mexico. But for shippers, it also saves considerable headaches. The parties need only handle one set of documents, and one electronic administration/tracking system, and there is considerably less "red tape" and other issues in a fully integrated system like this.

It's fair to say that what CPKC has created with its MMX (Mexico Midwest Express) is a game-changer for the companies on either end of the line. It directly competes with trucking on the line while being a significantly cheaper option for companies. As the efficiencies ramp up, it's also likely that it will become even cheaper.

With the USMCA (the agreement governing US and Mexican trade), it means that CPKC has positioned itself to be one reason why this agreement works. Many companies are moving their manufacturing and services home from Asia—sometimes to the US, sometimes to Mexico. The distances here are shorter, no longer require sea freight, and can work directly with CPKC. With its new line, the company controls what is known as the Laredo Gateway, which has become the busiest railway crossing in all of North America. The mere fact of controlling this crossing is obviously a massive advantage for prioritizing its own trains and transports, and this network links to every single coast: in Vancouver, in the Pacific via Lazaro Cardenas, and the Gulf Coast in the US.

The prospects for growth and expansion are tendentially good based on the expansion from the aforementioned move of manufacturing to Mexico. I would argue that if this upside materializes from the Mexican side, CPKC is going to grow significantly—and the market seems to, based on revenue and earnings growth estimates, believe this to be the case.

For 2025, the company saw significant growth already. An 8% increase in operating income, solid operating ratios, and a 4% increase in revenues. The fact that the company managed a 4% increase in revenue and twice as much in operating income suggests operational leverage on the part of the company—confirmed by significant EPS growth. This is confirmed by the fact as well that CPKC did not do any buybacks during the 2025 fiscal year—another advantage/management upside that I see here. Unlike many of its peers, the company is aiming all of its CapEx and capital available (aside from the small, sub-1% dividend) at capacity expansion CapEx—such as projects like the Mexico Bridge, new terminals, and its technological expansion—with a look at assets like hydrogen locomotives and the like.

Management has reiterated its focus on growth, not capital returns. So if you invest in CPKC, you need to be "okay" with growth being the story here and not capital returns. That also means that the company's dividend is quite unlikely to grow significantly here. Buybacks also won't come back—at least not until the merger debt, which currently is still there, is down to below its leverage target. Its current target is about 2.75x, and the company seems to be able to meet this within the next year or so.

However, what I want to focus on here is the fact that for many years, the absence of buybacks means that the EPS and OI growth relative to revenue growth has been organic, and confirmation of the company's operating leverage. This means that, provided it continues, there's significant upside here.

The company's going outlook for 2026 includes a continuation of the already-delivered AEPS growth, with CapEx at about $2.65B.

My forecast is that the company will be able to deliver at least 5-6% of continued, annual revenue growth (which also is in line with the company's own estimates), which, based on its operational leverage, comes to double-digits of AEPS growth—also in line with most estimates here—while maintaining a CapEx of at least $2.5B, but more likely $2.8B per year. Based on cited increases in trade, I believe there really to be only one direction for the company to go—and that direction is up.

I believe the company is one of the most attractively-positioned and capitalized railway companies on the entire market—unfortunately for investors, the price that this company trades at might not be attractive for investment, even with this considered—and here is why.

CPKC Valuation - Upside is tricky at this valuation, even with the growth

The current multiples for the company are at about 23x P/E. That's above the 20-year average of 20.7x and somewhat below the 5-year average of 24x. A higher growth rate would favor a valuation multiple closer to the higher end of the premium, but the company would have to, over time, trade close to 24 P/E to be considered a good investment, because at 24x P/E with the current growth estimates, we barely eke out a 15% annualized rate of return.

Part of the problem is the current shareholder returns. A 0.81% dividend just isn't that attractive, and it's unlikely to grow fast. This is a company for a very patient shareholder, and you really have to consider the company highly. About 3-4 months ago, when the company traded at sub-$100/share prices, I had no issue buying the company because the upside was clearly in the 14-18% annualized range, even in the low 20s in terms of P/E.

However, the farther above $100/share it goes, the slimmer that upside becomes, and the less attractive I'd say that the company, in turn, becomes.

This is a company that I want to be able to invest in, because I believe personally in its growth story and upside. It's tricky, however, to allow for a massive premium like this, because when looking at risks, there are a few of them to consider.

On the regulatory side, we have the review of the USMCA. It's not something that CPKC controls, but it's scheduled for about 3 months from now. The current political administration has made it clear that it seeks to address what it views as "imbalances" in US trading, specifically in the energy and automotive industries. These represent significant segments for CPKC. Any increase in protectionism would impact the company's infrastructure value and needs to be considered carefully. The effects of the mere threat of it have already resulted in lower investments in the Mexican manufacturing sector. If this continues, it could impact the company's EPS growth—and a 4-5% growing company cannot be argued to be worth 20-25x P/E. In that scenario, CPKC would need to be traded lower.

There are also Mexican regulatory issues that are too often overlooked. The current political climate in Mexico calls for a 40-hour workweek. If brought through, it could meaningfully increase CPKC costs, and require significant hiring—the minimum wages addressed about 4 months ago have already seen impacts on the company.

Also, following this massive merger, I would argue that the regulatory climate in the US has hardened. The STB, which is the regulator for CPKC, has already noted the company for service disruptions, and the risk here is if the company doesn't manage its existing operations, its contracts and traffic may be forced, by federal mandate, to be given to the competition.

So the current forecast and growth estimate are unimpacted by these risks. To properly account for these risks, I would argue we should moderate our expectations—discounting growth by about 20%. This impacts our price target and upside for the company, driving the rate of annualized return to about 11-12% per year, unless we buy the company at below $100/share.

I therefore put CPKG, the native ticker CP on the Canadian stock market, at a CAD price target of $100/share. Below this is attractive. That translates to an NYSE target (USD) of about $72.26/share, which at this time makes the company a "HOLD," not a "BUY."

At my IG, Wolf of Value, I look at other infrastructure and industrial companies with better upside and yield, which could be better options for the value-oriented investor.

Thesis

CPKG is a company that I want to buy and own, based on unmatched and attractive infrastructure assets in the railway industry. However, I don't believe the market is properly accounting for the risk involved here. Based on the company's estimate accuracy, fundamentals, and yield, I would put a $100/share price target to clear my hurdle of 15% annualized, which the company is not doing.

I would, based on the current company share price and estimates, give the company a "HOLD" rating and wait until we see a better valuation.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics)

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a price target of $100/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's not attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of April 2026.

data.title: March CPI Inflation - Ugly Overall, But Some Good News Inside
data.description:
By David W. Berson, Ph.D.

The March CPI jumped by 0.9 percent (equal to market expectations), bringing the 12-month trend rate up to 3.3 percent – the fastest pace since September 2023. This was largely an energy event, with energy prices up by 10.9 percent for the month (gasoline spurting by 21.3 percent and fuel oil by 30.7 percent). Fortunately, food prices were unchanged for the month, suggesting that there was not much linkage of higher energy prices into food costs (from, for example, rising fertilizer costs). The core CPI (removing the volatile food and energy components) rose by 0.2 percent, a bit less than market expectations. Even with modestly less core inflation than expected for the month, the trend rate rose from 2.5 percent (actually 2.47 percent, so almost 2.4) to 2.6 percent.

The good news in the report is that it is difficult to see much, if any, spillover from the jump in energy costs into other prices – at least not yet. The longer energy prices remain elevated, however, the more likely it is that other prices will be boosted – pushing the core CPI (and the core PCE price index, which the Fed follows more closely) higher. Yesterday’s release of the February PCE inflation data showed that this broader measure of inflation remained stuck at around 3.0 percent. The Fed will almost certainly stay on the sidelines until the full impact of the surge in energy prices – and their spillover into broader inflation – is clearer. The longer energy price gains are outsized, the more likely it is that they will leak into other parts of the economy, pushing core inflation higher. How high and for how long would it take for the Fed to tighten monetary policy? Too soon to tell. And what happens if the reduction in consumer purchasing power from higher energy prices slows economic growth – a negative supply shock that could push the economy into recession (although that is still not the most likely course at this time)? Would the Fed fight the higher inflation at the cost of slowing economic growth still more, or would it fight slower growth by easing policy at the cost of keeping inflation higher for longer? There are no easy answers in that scenario.

While this CPI report is for March, gasoline price data for April suggest that next month’s CPI will also show a jump. The real question will be whether core inflation also starts to move higher as a result of the energy price shock.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: SPPP: Not Sure About PGMs, And Certainly Not About Physical Trusts
data.description:
The Sprott Physical Platinum and Palladium Trust (SPPP) (SPPP:CA) is a closed-end fund that can give investors exposure to platinum and palladium, which together effectively encompass the market price of PGMs, used primarily in the catalytic converters of cars as their majority application, primarily palladium, whereas at least platinum has some more industrial uses.

Our position on the SPPP is the following:

  • Because of exposure to PGM prices, we are cautious, as we think there are threats to the direction of PGMs due to the Middle Eastern geopolitical situation.
  • As equity researchers and value investors, we cannot countenance, frankly almost ever, buying a physical trust for a commodity over buying a business that mines that commodity, which can operationally add value and provide a productive yield, particularly when physical trusts carry high fees to keep custody over the physical assets.

On both those counts, our position is not to get involved in PGM bets, as we find the geopolitical drivers of the price currently too unpredictable and don't want to take a position one way or another due to the risks and the volatility of the commodity.

Breakdown

The physical trust has a management fee of 1.01%. This is quite normal for physical trusts and is higher than diversified exposures in the form of ETFs, which usually carry lower passive fees. Due to the need to have physical custody of a commodity, the expenses are higher. Discussing the matter of how to get exposure to PGMs, we'd immediately argue that miners, such as Valterra Platinum Limited (ANGPY), make more sense since they are a productive company, adding value operationally through asset development, and pay a dividend, albeit highly variable based effectively on PGM prices. Therefore, it provides the same exposure but also the capacity to compound value as an operating mining business, an established one at that. The performance bears out the advantage, too.

The downside of Valterra over a physical trust of the commodity is that there is the differential risk of Valterra being a South Africa-based miner. While not an immediate concern, South Africa has a higher political risk of things like asset appropriation and arbitrary changes in concession charges and regulation, for example, in labour regulation, which could independently affect Valterra but not a physical trust and the price of the underlying commodity, since it's an operational thing and not a PGM market dynamic. Still, as investors and business owners, we are much more comfortable with Valterra over a quite high management fee physical trust, particularly with Valterra not having this issue at the moment, and also achieving solid cost savings.

Then there's the discussion of the PGM market:

Bull Case

The bull case would be:

  • ICE bans are untenable in Europe economically and industrially to some extent, and have backpedaled after the Ukraine war demonstrated how much we need fossil fuels. In the US, the Trump administration has been moving against authorities that may restrict emissions further in passenger vehicles. In general, there have been downward revisions in BEV demand curves. ICEs and the catalytic converters used in them, and driving PGM demand, seem like they could be here to stay longer than expected.
  • Maybe the Iran war will properly end. The ceasefire is running into political issues, but once the energy crisis ends, there is no more imminent reason to consider reducing household dependence on ICE vehicles, and also demand destruction for leveraged purchases will cease as disinflation becomes possible once again.

Bear Case

While we are not necessarily leaning towards the case, we want to say:

  • It's still very unclear whether the war will be resolved. Iran's demands are significant and may not be acceptable. Furthermore, regardless of how the war ends, there is uncertainty whether Iran will continue to control the Strait and institute its significant tolls that put a floor on the oil price. Additionally, there is a risk of other future oil crises, perhaps again around the Strait, with new threats and anxieties in the region now having emerged. The reinflation issue is a demand destruction risk for all purchases, especially leveraged ones like cars in general.
  • Then there is also the fact that there's been anecdotal evidence of the adoption of EVs happening as a consequence of higher gas prices at the pump, at least higher inquiries at certain dealerships. It is possible that this also accelerated renewable adoption in general, though that's not what happened in the last energy crisis around Ukraine, but there are new geopolitical fault lines; for example, maybe the EU cannot count on the advantage of allied US oil as much and may want to accelerate its renewable development.

Bottom Line

The bottom line is we don't want a physical trust with its fees; we'd rather go for Valterra for our PGM exposure. The other thing is that PGM demand, or specifically platinum and palladium demand, is around 65%+ driven by catalytic converters as the end market. The view even by the miners is that a surplus is coming, maybe in 2028, but some analysts say as soon as this year, and at any rate, growth in car demand will depend a lot on the end of a war that is threatening to re-raise rates and reinflate the economy. The war may also trigger more interest in renewables and EVs that may persist in the future.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Byrna Technologies: Conversion Collapse Is The Real Problem, Not Demand
data.description:
Byrna Technologies Inc. (BYRN) has fallen 54% since my last report. I have a Buy rating for the manufacturer of non-lethal launchers and safety equipment, but the reality is that the company is facing substantial headwinds from its sales mix. In this report, I discuss the company’s Q1 2026 earnings and update my price target.

Byrna Fails To Translate Sales Growth To Profit Growth

During the first quarter, sales rose 11% to $29 million, missing analyst estimates by $0.7 million. Revenues rose 11%, driven by continued sales expansion in the dealer and chain store channels, partially offset by post-holiday moderation and some quarter-end backlog slipping into the next quarter. Gross profit rose 9% to $17.4 million, but this indicates a margin decrease from 61% to 60% as the lower-margin dealer and chain store channels made up a larger portion of the sales, and those margins are lower compared to the e-commerce sales channel. Operating expenses rose 16% to $16.5 million, driven by higher market costs and costs in support of the expansion of the retail distribution channel. As a result, pre-tax income declined from $1.8 million to $0.8 million.

A Weak Outlook And e-Commerce Conversion

Byrna Technologies has lost 31% of its value since announcing its earnings. The reason is two-fold. First, we note that Q1 showed that higher revenues are not translating to higher profits. Secondly, CEO Conn Davis indicated during the earnings call that Q2 sales will be materially lower:

Looking ahead to Q2, I have spent my first week on the job aligned around one clear objective, winning the fight for revenue while simultaneously building the long-term foundation. Based on what we are seeing today, fiscal Q2 is developing materially below our expectations and below both the year-over-year and sequential improvement, we would ultimately expect this business to deliver. Part of that reflects a tougher comparison against last year's fiscal second quarter, which benefited from the CL launch and initial load-in orders with new retail partners of roughly $2.7 million.

Management points at the timing of products last year, but overall conversion rates in their high-margin e-commerce channel are down. The company is attracting stable traffic, but conversion is down materially.

There are various reasons for that. Some of the sales potential is shifting from high-margin e-commerce to lower-margin retail in-store sales. So, it could be that people go to the website of Byrna, check out a product, and then go to the store to buy the product. For Byrna, that is a lower margin sale. Furthermore, the audience is shifting from gun enthusiasts and gun owners to a wider audience, but the website is not built for that, and comparing several of Byrna’s solutions against each other is difficult. That may either mean that potential customers do not buy at all or they go to the store to win information and buy in-store. Further, the company is not effectively upselling.

So, the funnel is broken at the bottom, it seems. However, we also note that changes such as leveraging AI for ad generation may have a large reach but may result in the wrong audience being attracted to the site. Furthermore, the low conversion, which basically seems to be the result of a new audience coming to a website built for gun owners and enthusiasts, may also provide an indicator that the latter audience is highly saturated. So, there is a conversion gap there.

Byrna Technologies Remains A Buy, But Management Needs To Execute Better

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

The weaker outlook for Q2 is definitely disappointing, and from the earnings call, I also get the sense that management was caught off guard by the conversion gap in their high-margin business. So, they really have to streamline the site for better audience matching and conversion and also figure out the interference dynamics between the e-commerce side of the business and their retail channel. That has spared a complete rerating on my valuation window for Byrna Technologies, and at current prices there is no upside for the stock for this year, but 58% for next year. So, the very high-risk target would be around $10, implying nearly 60% upside. That is below the prior base case of $18.19. The valuation model would normally imply a strong buy rating, but I have added a manual point-based override in my rating for Byrna to account for the weaker conversion in their high-margin channel, and that would bring the rating to buy.

With a weaker second quarter and a shift in the sales distribution, I do expect that margins will dip this year. Against a lower expected CapEx figure and an improving operating cash flow reflecting inventory burn-off, there should be a meaningful uplift in the free cash flow performance. By 2027, the margins should be largely recovered, but the working capital and inventory tailwind that I expect for 2026 is not occurring again, leading to lower free cash flow expectations. The company has no bank debt, which is great, but execution is what matters now.

Conclusion: Byrna Technologies Needs To Realign And Dissolve Channel Interference

For Byrna, it seems that demand is not the problem, but the company has a major conversion headwind in positioning itself for a wide audience in the e-commerce business, which leads to low conversion and either a loss of sales or the sales shifting from the high-margin channel to lower-margin channels. That is something the company has now found out, and it needs to address that. So, the demand and upside are there for BYRN, but there also is execution risk.

data.title: The Storm Has Passed For Tilly's
data.description:
Just over a year ago, I decided to revisit Tilly's, Inc. (TLYS), a rather interesting company that focuses on selling casual apparel, footwear, accessories, and certain hard goods. In general, this is not a space that I find to be the most attractive. It is heavily competitive, and margins have a tendency to be low. But even by the standards of the space, Tilly's was facing some issues. Declining revenue and significant losses and cash outflows proved problematic. And I argued that there were better opportunities for market participants. At the end of the day, I ended up reaffirming the company as a ‘sell’ candidate. Since then, the stock has been virtually flat while the S&P 500 is up 14%. This certainly matches my expectations for a bearish rating. After all, just because I give a company a bearish rating does not mean I expect the share price to drop. Instead, it just means that I expect the stock to underperform the market for the foreseeable future.

Having said that, since I originally turned bearish about the business in April of 2023, shares have fallen drastically. They are currently down 53.1% at a time when the S&P 500 is up 50.5%. This is despite the fact that the firm has a tremendous amount of cash on hand relative to its overall market size. And it can be attributed to continued weakness from an operational standpoint. Management does expect, however, that the picture will change this year. They are actually forecasting rather meaningful revenue growth even as the number of locations that the business has in operation declines. Uncertainty still exists here. However, the picture is safe enough to justify an upgrade to a speculative ‘hold’ rating.

Tilly's is getting a little more fashionable

Over the last three years, Tilly's has faced some serious issues. From 2023 through 2025, as an example, revenue for the company dropped from $623.1 million to $553.6 million. This decline was driven by a couple of factors. After seeing comparable store sales drop by 10.6% in 2023, they fell a further 8% in 2024. We did, fortunately, see a small increase of 0.3% in 2025, so at least in that regard, we are seeing signs of stabilization. But the bigger issue for the business has been a decline in the number of locations that it has in operation. From 2023 through 2025, that number fell quite sharply from 249 to 223. Management has been working hard to shut down underperforming locations as it pushes toward profitability once again. It is worth noting that even though the number of locations in operation has dropped, the firm is seeing a bit of resilience when it comes to its e-commerce sales. They dropped only 1.8% year over year compared to the 3.1% drop that the business saw when it came to its physical stores.

We have seen a bit of progress in this regard. After seeing its net loss expand from $34.5 million in 2023 to $46.2 million in 2024, the company reported an improvement in its net loss to $17.5 million in 2025. Even though the decline in revenue would normally hurt a business like this, the firm enjoyed meaningful cost reductions. As an example, its cost of goods sold, excluding rent paid to a related party, improved from 73.06% in 2024 to 69.62% in 2025. I know that doesn't seem like much of a change. But when applied to the revenue generated in 2025, that's an extra $19 million in pre-tax savings. According to management, the company benefited from higher initial markups on its merchandise, as well as lower markdowns. More current inventory made this possible. On top of this, the firm enjoyed a decrease in buying, distribution, and occupancy costs, thanks to a net reduction in its store count of 17 locations. Meanwhile, selling, general, and administrative costs did rise in relation to revenue. But that was not enough to offset the improvements on the gross profit side of things.

Other profitability metrics are showing some signs of improvement as well. From 2024 to 2025, the firm’s operating cash flow went from negative $42 million to positive $4.1 million. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an improvement from negative $28.9 million to negative $4.1 million. Finally, we did see some weakness when it came to EBITDA, with that metric contracting from $9.9 million to $5 million. So not everything is perfect across the board.

Management has not provided detailed guidance when it comes to 2026 in its entirety. But they did provide some insights into the turnaround progress of the business, as well as what they think will happen for the first quarter. They stated, for instance, that they saw a 20% comparable net sales increase year over year in February of this year. The business is continuing to benefit from certain infrastructure investments dedicated to a price optimization tool, as well as warehouse management software that has already paid off in the form of certain cost savings. Investments in an AI-driven merchandise allocation tool have also been announced. The entire goal here seems to be centered around improving merchandise assortments, investing more heavily in digital marketing, and introducing changes to the overall brand. On top of this, while the number of locations in operation will drop from 238 in the first quarter of 2025 to 220 in the first quarter of this year, management did reveal that they expect to open between four and six new locations throughout the fiscal year in its entirety.

As a result of these efforts, management expects revenue for the first quarter of 2026 to range between $119 million and $125 million. That would be between 16% and 22% above what the company saw at the same time in 2025. But outside of this, we don't really know too much of what to expect. This does make it difficult to really value the company, especially in light of the fact that financial performance has been so weak as of late.

For instance, if we were to value the company on an EV-to-EBITDA basis using 2025 figures, it would be trading at a multiple of 16.3. That is certainly overvalued for a company with this track record. However, the changes that the firm is making should improve profitability by some degree. In the chart above, I calculated how much adjusted operating cash flow the company would need to generate to be fairly valued at a price-to-adjusted operating cash flow multiple of between 5 and 10. I did the same thing for EBITDA regarding the EV-to-EBITDA multiple. These are not unrealistic numbers. For instance, using 2025 sales figures as an example, the firm would need an EBITDA margin of between 1.48% and 2.94% to hit this range. That is entirely within the realm of possibility, especially given the improvements that the company has exhibited.

To be honest with you, while analyzing the company, I came very close to reaffirming it as a ‘sell’ candidate only because I would like to see more improvement before becoming neutral. However, after seeing that comparable sales jumped 10.3% in the final quarter of 2025 while e-commerce comparable sales grew 9.8%, I became a bit more optimistic. It also helps that the firm has no debt on its books and enjoys a cash position of $46.3 million. For a business with a market capitalization today of $128 million, that is massive. And it certainly reduces the risk profile of the company and gives it some runway to work with.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that is available right now, it is my belief that Tilly's makes for an interesting prospect. It's not one that I am ready to turn bullish on just yet. But I do think that, given the changes that we have seen and the strength of its balance sheet, upgrading it to a speculative ‘hold’ is appropriate.

data.title: Super Micro Computer: Cheap For A Reason
data.description:
It's been almost a year since our previous article on Super Micro Computer (SMCI) was released. Back then, we argued that the company's valuation was detached from the business's fundamentals. Our model back then gave Super Micro an intrinsic value of $25.47 per share at a time when its stock was trading much higher. Today, the stock is trading around $23 per share, and at first glance, it may seem that the main share price swing has already taken place.

However, if we look closely at the latest developments, we'll see that the key risks have increased and also become more structural in recent months. In addition, the relatively weak earnings results and the overall changing sentiment surrounding semiconductor stocks made us even more skeptical about Super Micro. That is why we continue to rate the company's stock as a Sell since the downside seems to be greater than we expected last year.

The Exposure to a Limited Number of Customers Is a Problem

Before diving deep into our bearish thesis, it is worth explaining the nature of growth that Super Micro has been demonstrating. At first, Super Micro really looks like one of the beneficiaries of the AI boom. Since the demand for AI-optimized servers remains high, the company has been able to quickly occupy its niche as a flexible solution integrator. As a result, its revenue has been growing at a rapid pace, and this has created the impression of a strong growth story.

But the key problem is that this growth is not stable in the long run. It depends heavily on the capital costs of a few large customers. In fact, the business is tied to the investment cycles of hyperscalers and large AI players. This means that revenue visibility remains low, while the volatility is high. When demand is shaped not by the end user but by the budgets of a few large corporations, any slowdown in investment can be reflected instantly in the results. And this is the fundamental weakness of the Super Micro business model.

Margin Issues Remain

Despite the rapid growth of revenues, the company does not show a proportional improvement in profitability. And this is not a coincidence but a consequence of its position in the value chain. Super Micro does not control key technologies and does not have its own product with high added value. It works as an integrator that collects solutions from components of other manufacturers.

Under such a model, it is very difficult to create a stable competitive advantage. Super Micro is forced to compete at the expense of price, speed, and flexibility, but not at the expense of unique technology. This means that margins remain under constant pressure, especially during periods of increased competition. The latest data only confirm this thesis. Despite the growth in volumes, operational leverage does not work as expected by the market. Margin does not expand and sometimes even decreases due to price competition and rising costs. This is a critical point that is often ignored by the bullish thesis.

The AI Infrastructure Cycle Is Not Endless

Another problem with Super Micro is that it's heavily exposed to the AI infrastructure cycle. Today, the market behaves as if this cycle of constant record capital spending will continue indefinitely. It makes sense to think that since the tech companies are investing tens of billions of dollars in data centers, the demand for servers remains extremely high. But historically, such phases always end the same way. First, there is an aggressive increase in capacities. Then there is a saturation phase. Finally, there's a normalization or even a decline in investments.

It can already be seen that the scale of capital expenditure has reached levels that are difficult to maintain in the long term. For Super Micro, this means direct risk. Its business has no protection in the form of recurring income or intellectual property. It depends on the volume of new orders. And once the investment cycle begins to slow down, it will immediately reflect on revenue and profits.

An additional risk is that large customers can change the supply structure. Some of them are already trying to work directly with manufacturers or develop their own solutions. In such a scenario, the role of integrators such as Super Micro may gradually decrease.

Other Major Risks to Consider

After the latest depreciation of Super Micro's shares, it may seem that the bottom has been reached. But we do not agree with this. The market is still laying out a relatively optimistic scenario in which high growth rates persist and margins remain stable. In our model, which is presented later in this article, we use more conservative assumptions. We take into account the normalization of growth after peak demand, the subsequent pressure on margins, and the increased level of risk. We also use a more realistic cost of capital, which reflects the cyclical nature of the business. As such, we do not see a sufficient upside that would justify an investment in Super Micro's stock. On the contrary, the balance of risk and potential return remains negative.

The Real Value of Super Micro

To estimate Super Micro's intrinsic value, we made a valuation model when its stock was trading at $22.67 per share at the time of the analysis. The model assumes a constant growth rate of 2.0%, which is generally consistent with our long-term assumptions for tier-2 semiconductor companies. Our forecast covers the next five years and is based on data from the company's most recent earnings report, including cash balances and total debt, allowing us to assess the valuation sensitivity under more normalized growth conditions.

The model applies a discount rate of 9.45%, which we figured out after calculating Super Micro's cost of equity and cost of debt. The cost of debt was estimated primarily using trailing twelve-month data, while the cost of equity was calculated using the CAPM model, where the risk-free rate is 4.29%, a 1-year beta of 2.82, and a market return rate of 7.77%.

The sales forecast in the model assumes a decline in the growth rate, similar to our previous model. We also expect an operational margin of 5.5% in 2026 and a gradual decrease to 5.1% by 2030. The model also applies a 15% tax rate. Other financial assumptions remain mostly consistent with Super Micro's historical averages.

This model shows that Super Micro's enterprise value is $11.15 billion. After adding the cash balances and deducting the outstanding debt, the model showed Super Micro's equity value to be $9.99 billion, which translates to an intrinsic value of $16.68 per share. This represents a discount of approximately 28.14% from the current market price.

Risks to Our Bearish Thesis

Risks to our bearish thesis certainly exist. The biggest of them is that the AI boom can last longer than the market expects. If hyperscalers continue to invest aggressively in infrastructure for several more years, the demand for servers will remain high.

It also can't be ruled out that Super Micro might be able to improve its operational efficiency or move to more marginal products. If a company manages to establish itself as a greater partner within the AI infrastructure supply chain, then it could mitigate some risks described earlier in this article.

Final Thoughts

In the last year, the weaknesses of Super Micro's business have become more obvious. The company continues to have a high dependence on cyclical demand while at the same time has limited marginality and weak long-term revenue visibility. That is why we believe that after the latest depreciation, the current price still does not fully reflect all the risks. That is why we continue to rate Super Micro as a Sell.

data.title: Avalyn Pharma Begins IPO Process For Pulmonary Fibrosis Pipeline
data.description:
A Quick Take On Avalyn Pharma's IPO

Avalyn Pharma Inc. (AVLN) has filed to raise capital for its treatment pipeline development, according to an S-1 registration statement.

The company is advancing drug candidate treatments for pulmonary fibrosis conditions.

AVLN has had encouraging tolerance and efficacy results in its lead candidate's trials.

The firm also has a strong investor syndicate and top-tier quality management in its favor.

Avalyn Overview and Development Efforts

Avalyn is a clinical-stage biopharma developing targeted treatment candidates for interstitial lung diseases [ILDs] like pulmonary fibrosis.

Pulmonary fibrosis results in the scarring of the lungs, leading to a decline in lung function and poor quality of life for suffering patients.

AVLN is developing inhaled formulations of already-approved drugs of pirfenidone and nintedanib for treating progressive pulmonary fibrosis and idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis.

The current status of the company's drug candidate pipeline is shown here:

The firm's wholly owned lead candidate, AP01, is currently in Phase II trials and has 'demonstrated encouraging tolerability and clinical activity data…[with] low rates of side effects, including GI toxicities and liver enzyme elevations, which have been commonly seen with oral products. At 48 weeks, the high-dose AP01 achieved near stabilization of lung function, as measured by forced vital capacity, or FVC.' (Source: SEC)

Management is led by President and Chief Executive Officer Ms. Lyn Baranowski, who has been with the firm since October 2022 and was previously COO of Altavant, where she oversaw the development of therapies for rare respiratory diseases until the company's acquisition by Sumitomo Dainippon in 2019.

Avalyn's Market and Competitors

The global idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis [IPF] treatment market was an estimated $3.7 billion in 2024 and was expected to reach $5.5 billion by 2030, according to a market research report by Grand View Research.

If achieved, this growth would represent a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 6.8% from 2025 to 2030.

Since older individuals are more susceptible to developing chronic diseases like IPF, market demand is expected to grow as the global population continues to age, along with the increasing life expectancy of populations across the globe.

The chart below illustrates the historical and projected future growth expectations of the worldwide IPF treatment market by drug class through 2030:

In 2024, the North America region accounted for the highest market share, at 37.5%.

The Asia Pacific region is expected to produce the highest growth rate through 2030, at a CAGR of 7.8%.

By route of administration, orally administered medications had nearly 70% of the market in 2024.

Competitors with existing products or candidates in development include:

  • Legacy Pharma

  • Genentech/Roche (RHHBY)

  • Boehringer Ingelheim

  • United Therapeutics (UTHR)

  • Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY)

  • Insmed (INSM)

  • Celea Therapeutics

  • Vicore Pharma

  • Endeavor Biomedicines

  • Contineum Therapeutics (CTNM)

  • Eli Lilly and Co. (LLY)

  • Others

The company is also actively developing treatments for progressive pulmonary fibrosis, which is a smaller market size than idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, according to public research reports.

Avalyn's Financial Results

The firm's last two years of financial results are typical of a clinical-stage biopharma in that they feature significant R&D and G&A expenses associated with its pipeline development efforts and no revenue.

As of December 31, 2025, Avalyn had $138.4 million in cash, equivalent and marketable securities, and $15.3 million in total liabilities.

The company's investor syndicate had invested a fair market value total of $399 million in investment as of the end of 2025.

IPO Details

AVLN has filed to offer shares of its common stock and has used a placeholder figure of $100 million for purposes of the SEC filing review fee calculation, although the final amount may be higher.

Shareholder sale lock-ups will be for the typical 180-day period from the effective date of the prospectus, as determined by the SEC.

AVLN said it intends to utilize the IPO net proceeds as described below:

to advance development of AP01 through Phase 2b topline data and into Phase 3 development;

to advance development of AP02 through Phase 2 topline data and into Phase 3 development;

to advance development of AP03 through clinical development and Phase 1 topline data; and

the remainder for research and development activities for additional programs, working capital and general corporate purposes.

(Source: SEC)

Management said the company is not currently a party to any legal proceedings that it believes would have a material adverse effect on its business, operations, or financial condition.

The listed underwriters of the IPO include Morgan Stanley, Jefferies, Evercore ISI, and Guggenheim Securities, although additional entities may be added later, at management's discretion.

My Thoughts About Avalyn's IPO

Avalyn is seeking public capital as it enters the heart of its clinical development, with two candidates in trials and one preparing to enter Phase 1 trials.

The company's lead candidate, a different formulation of an existing, approved drug, has produced positive tolerability and efficacy in trials to date, so the firm's outlook is positive in this regard.

The overall market for ILD treatments is large and expected to grow at a moderate rate in the coming years, although it features a number of major players and several potential new entrants, such as Avalyn, who are seeking to further develop treatment options for patients.

The company's investor syndicate includes a number of well-known life science venture capital firms and strategic investors, so its investor base is quite strong and a positive asset for the firm.

Avalyn is well-capitalized, and it will need to be further capitalized not only with the IPO's net proceeds but likely additional capital infusions afterward, as it is still relatively early in its pipeline development and entering the most cash-intensive part of its development lifecycle.

Management is also a strong suit for the company, with C-suite officers all having extensive life science industry experience in their respective employment histories.

As to risks, the biggest risk would be trial-related, whether not meeting trial endpoint expectations or having material adverse events among the trial patients.

The company is also using the 'smaller reporting company' and 'emerging growth company' designations, which allow management to produce less information for shareholders.

Many of these types of company stocks have performed poorly post-IPO, although they tend to be concentrated in general technology sectors and not in the life sciences.

Also, the biopharma sector has seen highly bifurcated performance post-IPO over the last few years, with some firms performing very well and others quite poorly, so the biopharma IPO market has been volatile and difficult to predict.

And reformulation of existing products may not produce a superior outcome with enough of a differentiation from the standard of care.

Management's pricing and valuation expectations will be important for prospective life science investors in the IPO.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be determined
